text: {"matches":[{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":38,"rationale":"Both give the cholesterol amount in a large egg, but differ in reported value (200 mg vs 186 mg) and scope (yolk vs whole egg)."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":26,"rationale":"Claim 0 provides a specific cholesterol amount, while claim 26 makes a general statement that eggs are high in cholesterol."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":38,"toIndex":26,"rationale":"Claim 38 gives a precise cholesterol amount, which is a specific instance of the general claim 26 that eggs are naturally high in cholesterol."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":3,"toIndex":1,"rationale":"Claim 3 specifies that dietary cholesterol has a smaller effect than dietary fats for most people, a more detailed version of the general weak relationship expressed in claim 1."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":27,"toIndex":3,"rationale":"Claim 27 concerns the effect of egg cholesterol specifically, whereas claim 3 addresses cholesterol from foods in general; both can be true for their respective scopes."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":27,"toIndex":1,"rationale":"Claim 27 focuses on eggs, while claim 1 addresses dietary cholesterol overall; the statements are compatible but refer to different scopes."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":31,"rationale":"Both state that up to one egg per day (≈ seven per week) does not increase heart disease risk in healthy individuals."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":54,"rationale":"Claim 2 limits the outcome to heart disease risk, whereas claim 54 includes a broader set of cardiovascular outcomes (heart attack, stroke, etc.)."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":31,"toIndex":54,"rationale":"Claim 31 mentions only heart disease risk, while claim 54 covers heart attack, stroke, and other cardiovascular diseases."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":34,"toIndex":6,"rationale":"Claim 34 specifies a weekly intake of seven eggs for diabetics, a stricter case of the daily‑egg consumption described in claim 6."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":6,"toIndex":28,"rationale":"Claim 6 reports increased heart disease risk among diabetics eating eggs, a subset of the general link between eggs and heart disease reported in claim 28."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":34,"toIndex":35,"rationale":"Claim 34 suggests increased heart disease risk for diabetics eating seven eggs weekly, while claim 35 reports no connection in the same population."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":6,"toIndex":35,"rationale":"Claim 6 finds increased risk for diabetics eating eggs daily, whereas claim 35 finds no risk association for diabetics."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":28,"toIndex":29,"rationale":"Claim 28 links eggs to heart disease, while claim 29 attributes most of the risk to foods commonly eaten with eggs; both can be true for different contributors."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":28,"toIndex":30,"rationale":"Claim 28 associates eggs with heart disease, whereas claim 30 emphasizes cooking method as the primary risk factor; the statements are compatible but focus on different mechanisms."},{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":46,"rationale":"Both claim a weak or non‑appreciable relationship between dietary cholesterol and serum cholesterol, differing only in wording."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":47,"toIndex":1,"rationale":"Claim 47 reports that an egg a day raises LDL cholesterol, contradicting claim 1’s assertion of only a weak relationship."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":47,"toIndex":46,"rationale":"Claim 47 indicates a measurable LDL increase, opposing claim 46’s statement of no appreciable relationship."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":48,"toIndex":1,"rationale":"Claim 48 says most studies show eggs raise blood cholesterol, conflicting with claim 1’s weak‑relation claim."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":48,"toIndex":46,"rationale":"Claim 48’s finding of cholesterol‑raising effects opposes claim 46’s conclusion of no appreciable relationship."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":55,"toIndex":57,"rationale":"Both provide the same protein (≈ 6 g) and calorie (72 kcal) content for a large egg."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":58,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"Both report that up to one egg per day does not increase heart attacks, strokes, or other cardiovascular disease rates in large prospective cohorts."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":58,"toIndex":54,"rationale":"Both state that consuming up to one egg per day does not raise risk of heart attack, stroke, or other cardiovascular diseases."}]}
